answer.text:
## Article 47B procedure to set aside a trustee decision for mistake

The Jersey mistake jurisdiction is **statutory**, sitting in **Articles 47B–47H of the Trusts (Jersey) Law 1984**, inserted by the Trusts (Amendment No. 6) (Jersey) Law 2013. Article 47B is the **definitional gate** — it defines what counts as a "mistake" — and **Article 47G** is the operative provision for setting aside a trustee's (or other fiduciary's) exercise of a power. Article 47E performs the parallel function for transfers of property *into* a trust (`jersey/trusts/article-47-set-aside.md`).

**What Article 47B defines as a "mistake"** — the ground is deliberately wide. It covers a mistake as to:

- the **effect** of the transaction;
- its **consequences** (expressly including tax consequences);
- any **advantage** the person believed they would gain; or
- any **fact existing before or at the time of** the disposition or power-exercise.

Article 47B also excludes testamentary dispositions from the regime (`jersey/trusts/article-47-set-aside.md`).

**The Article 47G route for a trustee decision.** A trustee, protector, beneficiary or other appropriate person may apply for the Royal Court to declare the **exercise of a fiduciary power voidable** on the ground that the power was exercised under mistake; if granted, the power-exercise is reversed and the position immediately before is restored — typically *ab initio*, with third-party rights acquired in good faith and for value before the declaration generally protected (`jersey/trusts/article-47-set-aside.md`).

**The three-limb test** (formulated in *In re S & T Trusts* [2015] JRC259 and applied since). The applicant must show:

1. there was a **mistake** within the wide Article 47B definition;
2. **but for** the mistake, the transfer or power-exercise would not have been made in the form it was; and
3. the mistake was **so serious** that it would be **just** for the Court to grant relief.

Failure on any one limb defeats the application; limb 2 is the most common ground of defeat (`jersey/trusts/article-47-set-aside.md`; `jersey/use-cases/trust-officer/article-47-set-aside-for-mistake.md`).

**Procedurally**, the application is by **Representation in the Royal Court, Samedi Division**, supported by affidavit evidence of the relevant state of mind, with the affected parties joined as respondents (trustee, beneficiaries, third parties who received property). Contemporaneous documentary evidence — file notes, board minutes, adviser correspondence — is critical; later reconstructions are weak. Trustee costs typically come from the fund; a settlor-applicant in a personal-mistake case generally bears their own (`jersey/use-cases/trust-officer/article-47-set-aside-for-mistake.md`).

## How Jersey is wider than *Pitt v Holt*

In **Pitt v Holt; Futter v Futter [2013] UKSC 26**, the English Supreme Court drew a line between:

- a **"mistake"** (an erroneous belief about a relevant fact or law), which can ground rescission; and
- **"mere causative ignorance"** or **"inadequate deliberation"**, which generally cannot.

The Royal Court, applying the **statutory** Jersey regime, has **rejected that distinction**. As summarised in the corpus: under Article 47E (and equally 47G), the Court asks only whether there was *any* mistake within the wide Article 47B definition — the **"manner" of the mistake is irrelevant**, so the English distinction between mistake and inadequate deliberation/mere ignorance is not imported (`jersey/trusts/article-47-set-aside.md`; `jersey/use-cases/trust-officer/article-47-set-aside-for-mistake.md`).

The practical width that follows:

- A Jersey mistake claim is **easier to make out** than its English counterpart — a trustee or settlor who simply did not turn their mind to a consequence can still be within the Article 47B gate, where on the English side they would fall on the "inadequate deliberation" side of the *Pitt v Holt* line.
- This is the **policy reason** an English adviser will sometimes prefer a Jersey-law trust where the principal risk being hedged is a future tax rule or other consequence the adviser cannot yet see clearly.
- The width is **not unlimited**: the Royal Court has declined relief where limb 2 fails (e.g., *In the Matter of the M Settlement*), where the mistake is really a commercial misjudgement, where the applicant seeks **partial rescission** that amounts to re-writing history, or where the alleged mistake is only the **loss of a hoped-for *future* tax advantage** rather than the suffering of an unforeseen charge (`jersey/trusts/article-47-set-aside.md`; `jersey/use-cases/trust-officer/article-47-set-aside-for-mistake.md`).

A final cross-border caveat from the corpus: the Jersey declaration is necessary but not sufficient — whether **foreign tax consequences** are unwound depends on whether the foreign revenue authority recognises the Royal Court's rescission as restoring the *status quo ante* (HMRC has historically done so for IHT/CGT; the IRS is more cautious) (`jersey/trusts/article-47-set-aside.md`).

(Both cited files have `last_verified: 2026-05-14`, well within the 180-day freshness window from today, 2026-05-19.)
